key_insights.insight: We can choose how we want to be in any given moment by understanding and utilizing the different parts of our brain.
key_insights.quote: So we have these four different anatomically, neuroanatomically structured parts of our brain, and we can pick and choose who and how we want to be in any moment when we know what our choices are.

key_insights.insight: Society often overvalues the left-brain's logical, analytical functions, leading to imbalance and a neglect of the right brain's present-moment awareness and connection.
key_insights.quote: We are skewed as a society to that left thinking portion of our brain... We're completely out of balance because we're at the balance of the value of that left brain.

key_insights.insight: The right hemisphere connects us to the present moment, joy, and a sense of belonging, while the left focuses on the individual and societal norms.
key_insights.quote: In the right hemisphere, you don't even exist. You exist as a part of it all. So what you hear gets integrated with what you smell, with what you feel, gets integrated in the excitement of possibility.

key_insights.insight: Trauma's purpose is to give us information and keep us safe; we cause problems when we turn the trauma into a lifestyle.
key_insights.quote: The purpose of trauma is to say to you, you're a biological creature... The trauma is supposed to be information. We get in trouble when we turn it into a lifestyle.

key_insights.insight: Emotions are meant to last only 90 seconds; prolonged feelings are usually the result of rethinking the initial thought.
key_insights.quote: From the moment you think of thought, we're only doing three things inside of our brain at any moment in time... But if you simply observe it, it will loop right through just like a muscle reflex. It's an emotional reflex, less than 90 seconds.

key_insights.insight: Each hemisphere processes information differently: the left learns facts and details, while the right learns context and the big picture.
key_insights.quote: When we learn, we learn facts and details with the left brain,but we learn context and big picture with the right brain.

key_insights.insight: Mental health is intricately linked to the well-being of brain cells and taking steps to ensure our brain cells are loved and nurtured is crucial.
key_insights.quote: Our mental health is 100% dependent on the health and well-being of the brain. And the health and well-being of the brain...is 100% dependent on the health and the well-being of the brain cells.

actionable_advice.advice: Actively practice shifting between the four 'characters' in your brain to master the moment.
actionable_advice.how_to: 1. Observe yourself to identify which character is dominant in different situations. 2. Identify what brings you joy. 3. Give yourself permission to engage in actions that give way to Character three to refuel your spirit.

actionable_advice.advice: Acknowledge and value trauma as information, not a lifestyle.
actionable_advice.how_to: Acknowledge the purpose trauma served, let yourself feel it, but consciously pull energy towards the other portions of your brain for self-soothing; use anger to advocate for others.

actionable_advice.advice: Prioritize brain health at a cellular level.
actionable_advice.how_to: Get sufficient sleep for cleaning cellular waste; consume fresh, organic foods and limit pesticides and sugar; move your body; hydrate.

actionable_advice.advice: Pause for 30 seconds to save your own life and be more mindful.
actionable_advice.how_to: Take a breath, relax, and be conscious, especially in potentially dangerous situations like driving. Remember that taking some time is not only good but nessecary for proper functioning

memorable_stories: Dr. Jill Bolte-Taylor's personal experience of having a stroke and losing function in the left hemisphere of her brain, and her subsequent 8-year recovery process.
Dr. Taylor describes her aunt who encouraged her to dissect roadkill, sparking her interest in anatomy.
contrarian_claims: Enjoy the emotion, even if you're angry. She argues that being capable of anger is important for setting healthy boundaries.
The way to heal trauma is not to get rid of it.
episode_title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - No.1 Brain Scientist: My Brain Shut Down & I Realised Everything I Believed Was Wrong!
